title: Narrow Normal-RAM Store Slot Address
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500 target.
The structural slot should therefore target a free win: improve the LUT/routing
shape without changing CoreMark cycles, branch behavior, memory ordering, or
the RVFI contract.

The one-entry pending-store slot is still worth keeping; deleting it exposed
dStall cycles and lost performance. However, the slot is allocated only for
legal normal-RAM stores below `0x00100000`. UART/timing-marker stores and any
high-address store are intentionally nonbufferable and wait on the live dmem
path. Despite that invariant, `store_slot.sv` stores and muxes a full 32-bit
address, so twelve high address bits are resettable state and dmem mux inputs
that are always zero whenever the slot owns the external port.

Prior structural payload-pruning attempts changed wider pipeline registers
and were placement-noisy. This hypothesis is narrower and tied to a hard
backend property already present in the design: the pending store slot is a
normal-RAM-only structure. Narrowing only its address payload should save a
small amount of state and high-bit dmem mux logic while preserving the exact
store buffering behavior that protects the current CoreMark score.
hypothesis:
Specialize the pending-store slot address to the normal RAM window. Change
the store-slot enqueue and drain address payload from 32 bits to the low
20 byte-address bits, because every buffered store has
`in.alu_result[31:20] == 12'b0`. Reconstruct the external drain address as
`{12'b0, store_slot_drain_addr_low}` only when the slot is valid. The live
MEM-stage dmem path continues to drive the full 32-bit address for loads,
ready stores, MMIO stores, and trapping/nonbufferable cases.

Keep the existing bufferability condition semantically unchanged: only valid,
legal, aligned normal-RAM stores may enqueue into the slot. The predicate may
be written as the existing `< 32'h0010_0000` comparison or as an equivalent
high-bit zero decode, but high-address stores must not allocate the slot.
Store data, write masks, drain priority, drain-on-ready behavior, MEM wait
generation, MEM/WB hold semantics, and RVFI memory reporting remain exactly
as today.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should be identical because no instruction stream,
replay predictor behavior, load-use interlock, forwarding path, branch/JALR
repair, divider protocol, store-slot scheduling rule, or memory handshake is
changed. The intended Pareto win is lower LUT4 and slightly less routing from
removing twelve constant-zero address bits from the store-slot register and
top-level dmem-address mux, with fitness flat and the already-met LUT target
improved.

changes.description:
Narrow the stored address payload and corresponding enqueue/drain ports
from 32 bits to 20 bits for normal-RAM byte addresses. Keep `valid`,
write data, write mask, enqueue priority, drain-ready handling, reset
behavior, and all one-entry ordering semantics unchanged. The slot should
continue to hold only one buffered store and should not gain any new
scheduling behavior.

Update the store-slot address wires to 20 bits. When `store_slot_valid`
owns the dmem port, drive `io_dmemAddr` as
`{12'b0, store_slot_drain_addr_low}` while continuing to drive the stored
write data and mask. When the slot is not valid, keep the live MEM-stage
dmem address/data/mask/read-enable wiring exactly as today. Preserve the
top-level `core` IO, RVFI NRET=2 port layout, channel-0 retirement rule,
channel-1 tie-off, and `rvfi_order` counter unchanged.

Change the store-slot enqueue address output to the low 20 bits of the
effective store address. Preserve the normal-RAM-only allocation guard so
the narrowed payload is valid by construction; high-address UART,
timing-marker, or out-of-window stores must remain nonbufferable live
requests. Do not change byte/halfword write replication, memory masks,
load lane muxes, misalignment traps, store-slot enqueue timing, dmem wait
behavior, MEM/WB fields, or RVFI aligned-memory reporting.

title: MRU-Free Hashed Replay Replacement
motivation:
The current selected RTL meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86 CoreMark
iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500 target. The
predictor slot therefore needs a free win: reduce predictor area or improve
IF timing without changing the benchmark instruction stream, replay hit
mechanism, RVFI contract, or memory behavior.

The experiment log shows that the two-bank IF replay predictor is worth
keeping: deleting it exposed too much random iStall penalty, while shrinking
tags or removing the candidate-PC guard hurt Fmax. The remaining low-risk
predictor state is the per-index MRU replacement bit. It is not part of the
architectural lookup key, does not affect correctness, and exists only to
choose a victim bank when both ways of a replay index are valid and the
current fill misses both tags.

CoreMark's useful replay locality should mostly come from hot low-RAM loops
and short call paths. For a 128-entry table indexed by pc[8:2], the cheapest
conflict discriminator is pc[9], the low bit of the stored high tag, because
it separates the two adjacent 512-byte regions that alias every table index.
Replacing the resettable MRU array with this deterministic tag-bit way choice
should remove 128 bits of predictor state, reset fanout, and fill-bank
update logic while preserving two-way lookup and the cold-fill use of both
banks.
hypothesis:
Delete the `replay_mru` array from `rtl/if_stage.sv` and make replay-table
replacement deterministic. The fill path should still update an already
matching bank first. If neither bank matches, it should prefer an invalid
bank so cold sets still populate both ways. Only when both ways are valid
and the fill tag misses should it choose the victim bank from a cheap tag
hash, specifically `replay_fill_tag_hi[0]` (pc[9]).

The lookup side remains fully two-way associative: both bank tags are still
compared against `replay_lookup_tag_hi`, live imem data still has priority,
the registered `replay_cand_pc_q == pc` guard still protects current-PC
replay use, and the same-entry fill bypass still supplies `imem_data` when
the just-fetched word is also the next registered candidate. Static
backward-branch prediction, aligned direct-JAL prediction, JALR EX
resolution, redirect priority, `fetch_ready`, and PC update semantics remain
unchanged.

CoreMark cycles should remain unchanged if the hot replay conflicts are the
common adjacent-512-byte aliases that pc[9] separates, while LUT4 and IF
reset/control fanout should drop slightly from removing the MRU storage and
update mux. The intended phase-2 result is strict Pareto improvement through
equal CoreMark iterations/cycle with lower LUT4 and/or higher Fmax. Top-level
IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0-only retirement, channel-1 tie-off, strict
`rvfi_order`, decoder legality, memory traps, pending-store behavior, and
the divider protocol remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Remove the `replay_mru` declaration, reset-loop assignment, and update.
Replace `replay_fill_bank` selection with match-first, invalid-bank-first,
then hashed replacement logic: bank 0 on a bank-0 tag match, bank 1 on a
bank-1 tag match, otherwise choose an invalid bank if either way is
invalid, otherwise select the victim from `replay_fill_tag_hi[0]`. Keep
both replay banks, full high-PC tags, valid bits, instruction payloads,
two-way lookup comparisons, `replay_cand_pc_q` equality guard,
fill-bypass behavior, live-imem priority, static branch/JAL prediction,
JALR-unpredicted behavior, redirect priority, and `fetch_ready` semantics
unchanged.

Update only the IF/ID replay predictor comments so they describe
deterministic tag-bit replay replacement instead of per-index MRU
replacement. Do not change bundle fields, control encodings, decoder
illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the
NRET=2 contract.

# Implementation Notes

## Changes made

- Removed the `replay_mru` array from `rtl/if_stage.sv`, including reset and fill-time update logic.
- Replaced replay fill-bank selection with deterministic replacement:
  - update bank 0 on a bank-0 tag match,
  - update bank 1 on a bank-1 tag match,
  - otherwise fill an invalid bank, preferring bank 0 before bank 1,
  - otherwise choose the victim bank from `replay_fill_tag_hi[0]` / `pc[9]`.
- Kept both replay banks, full high-PC tags, valid bits, payload storage, two-way lookup comparison, registered candidate-PC guard, fill bypass, live imem priority, prediction, redirect, and fetch-ready behavior unchanged.
- Updated IF replay predictor comments in `rtl/if_stage.sv` and `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to describe deterministic tag-bit replacement instead of per-index MRU replacement.

title: Resetless Valid-Guarded Microstate
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The accept rule is strict Pareto dominance, so this micro_opt slot
should aim for a no-cycle-change synthesis cleanup that trims reset fanout or
LUT mapping without disturbing the replay predictor, store buffering, branch
repair, or RVFI contract.

The remaining low-risk reset cleanup is in state whose payload is either
explicitly guarded by a valid bit or affects only replacement way choice. The
one-entry pending-store slot resets its address, data, and mask payload even
though the top-level dmem arbiter observes those fields only when `valid_q`
is set, and every enqueue overwrites the entire payload before asserting a
visible drain. Similarly, the IF replay predictor resets the 128 per-index
MRU bits even though both replay banks' valid bits are reset. A cold replay
set with both banks invalid may choose either bank first; the banks are
symmetric, and after fills the existing update logic reestablishes ordinary
MRU replacement.

A broader resetless divider-payload experiment reduced LUT4 but lost Fmax,
so this hypothesis deliberately avoids arithmetic datapaths and inter-stage
pipeline bundles. It targets only small valid-guarded control/payload state
that should not change CoreMark cycles and should not add any new muxing.
hypothesis:
Remove reset assignments from the pending-store slot payload registers while
keeping `valid_q` reset to zero. `addr_q`, `wdata_q`, and `wmask_q` should be
written only on enqueue as today and then used only when the valid bit gives
the store slot ownership of the dmem port. Reset must still make the slot
empty, so no resetless payload bit can drive `io_dmemAddr`, `io_dmemWData`,
or `io_dmemWEn` until after a complete legal buffered store has been
enqueued.

Also remove the reset assignment from the IF replay predictor's `replay_mru`
array while preserving the array and its update policy. Replay valid bits for
both banks remain reset to zero. On a cold set, the resetless MRU bit may
select either invalid way for the first fill, but because lookup compares
both banks and fill updates the MRU bit after every accepted instruction, this
is functionally equivalent to a bank-name swap. Live imem priority, two-bank
lookup, tag/instruction arrays, fill bypass, registered candidate PC guard,
static backward-branch prediction, aligned direct-JAL prediction, JALR EX
resolution, redirect priority, and `fetch_ready` semantics remain unchanged.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical: no PC decision, replay hit
rule after warm-up, load-use interlock, dmem wait rule, pending-store
scheduling rule, forwarding path, divider protocol, writeback rule, or
retirement rule changes. The intended Pareto win is a small LUT4 and/or Fmax
improvement from removing about two hundred reset-controlled state bits and
their reset fanout, while preserving the already-met area target and every
invariant in `CLAUDE.md`.

changes.description:
Keep `valid_q` reset to zero, but remove reset assignments from
`addr_q`, `wdata_q`, and `wmask_q`. Preserve enqueue priority,
drain-ready handling, drain output assignments, one-entry ordering, and
all module ports. The resetless payload must remain completely guarded by
`valid_q`, and every enqueue must still write the full address, write
data, and write mask before the slot can drain.

Remove `replay_mru[i] <= 1'b0` from the reset loop, leaving the MRU array
resetless while preserving its declaration and fill-time update
`replay_mru[replay_fill_idx] <= replay_fill_bank`. Keep both replay bank
valid arrays reset to zero, retain the existing fill-bank selection
expression, and do not change replay tags, instruction payloads, lookup
comparisons, fill bypass, registered candidate PC guard, live-imem
priority, branch/JAL prediction, redirect priority, PC update semantics,
or `fetch_ready`.

Update only comments if needed to document that replay MRU state is
resetless because bank valid bits define correctness after reset. Do not
change bundle fields, control encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI
field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

- Removed the reset assignment for each `replay_mru` entry in `rtl/if_stage.sv`; both replay-bank valid arrays still reset to zero and fill-time MRU updates are unchanged.

title: Resetless Pending Store Slot Payload
motivation:
The current selected RTL meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86 CoreMark iter/s
versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500 target. The structural
slot should therefore look for a free win: keep the CoreMark cycle count and
successful memory-ordering structure unchanged while shaving area or reset
fanout.

The one-entry pending-store slot is worth keeping because deleting it exposed
dStall cycles and lost performance. Its control state, however, is only a
single valid bit. The stored address, replicated write data, and byte mask are
completely overwritten on every enqueue and are consumed only while
`valid_q` is high. Today those 68 payload bits are still reset to zero, adding
reset muxing and reset fanout to a backend structure whose payload value is
architecturally irrelevant whenever the slot is invalid.

A resetless payload is a narrower structural cleanup than prior store-slot
rewrites, pipeline-payload pruning, or replay-table changes. It preserves the
proven store-slot scheduling rule and touches only state initialization that
is already guarded by validity.
hypothesis:
Make the pending-store slot payload registers resetless while keeping the
slot valid bit resettable. In `rtl/store_slot.sv`, reset must still clear
`valid_q` so the slot cannot own the dmem port after reset. Do not reset
`addr_q`, `wdata_q`, or `wmask_q`; every enqueue writes all three fields
before `valid_q` can make them observable at the top-level dmem mux.

The external behavior should be bit-identical. A legal aligned normal-RAM
store still enqueues only under the existing MEM-stage predicate, the slot
still drains only when `io_dmemReady` is high, drain priority over the live
MEM request is unchanged, and a simultaneous drain plus enqueue still keeps a
valid entry containing the newer store. While the slot is invalid, its payload
outputs may hold arbitrary resetless values, but `core.sv` must continue to
select the live MEM-stage dmem address/data/mask whenever `store_slot_valid`
is false.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should be unchanged because no fetch, decode,
execute, memory handshake, pending-store scheduling, writeback, or RVFI
retirement rule changes. The intended Pareto win is a small LUT4 reduction
and possibly slightly better backend placement from removing reset muxes on
the store-slot payload, with no regression to the already-met CoreMark target
and no weakening of any CLAUDE.md invariant.

changes.description:
Change reset handling so only `valid_q` is reset. Leave `addr_q`,
`wdata_q`, and `wmask_q` resetless, and continue assigning all payload
fields on every enqueue. Preserve the one-entry state machine, drain-fire
behavior, drain-plus-enqueue priority, module ports, and output assigns.

No behavioral topology change is intended. Audit the dmem arbitration to
keep `store_slot_valid` as the sole selector for store-slot payload
outputs, so resetless payload values are never exposed while the slot is
invalid. Preserve top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 fields, channel-0 retirement
ordering, channel-1 tie-off, and the live MEM-stage dmem path.

Keep the existing store-slot enqueue predicate and payload generation
unchanged: only valid, legal, aligned normal-RAM stores may enqueue, and
enqueue must provide full address, replicated write data, and byte mask.
Do not change load extraction, byte masks, misalignment traps, MEM wait
behavior, MEM/WB fields, RVFI memory reporting, or dmem request gating.

implementation_notes:
## Resetless Pending Store Slot Payload

### What changed

- Updated `rtl/store_slot.sv` so reset clears only `valid_q`.
- Left `addr_q`, `wdata_q`, and `wmask_q` resetless. They are still written together on every enqueue.
- Audited the existing top-level dmem arbitration in `rtl/core.sv`: store-slot payload outputs are selected only when `store_slot_valid` is true, otherwise the live MEM-stage dmem path is selected.

### Deviations

title: Prune Direct-JAL Repair Term
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The accept rule is strict Pareto dominance, so this predictor slot
should target a free win: simplify predictor repair logic without changing
the instruction stream, replay hit behavior, memory ordering, or RVFI
contract.

The current frontend always predicts aligned direct JALs in IF. A valid
direct JAL can enter ID/EX only from a non-killed IF payload, and for that
payload `jal_predict_taken` is true whenever the direct-JAL target is
word-aligned. If the direct-JAL target is misaligned, IF deliberately does
not predict it; EX then marks the instruction as trapping, suppresses the
redirect, clears the link write, and reports `pc+4` as the resolved next PC.

That makes the EX-side direct-JAL repair fallback redundant for the only
non-trapping direct-JAL case. A previous branch-only metadata experiment
removed this repair while also changing IF's exported prediction bit, and
that broader change was placement-noisy. This hypothesis is narrower: leave
IF metadata and direct-JAL prediction exactly as they are, and remove only
the unreachable EX repair term.
hypothesis:
In `rtl/ex_stage.sv`, delete the `jal_mispredict` wire and remove direct
JAL from `jump_redirect`. After this change, EX jump redirects should be
generated only for valid, non-misaligned JALR instructions. Conditional
branches continue to use the existing outcome-only repair
`branch_taken != in.predicted_taken`, and direct JALs continue to rely on
IF's existing aligned-target prediction.

Keep all direct-JAL datapath behavior unchanged: IF still predicts aligned
direct JAL targets, `out.predicted_taken` still includes direct-JAL
predictions, EX still computes `jump_target` for JAL and JALR, JAL/JALR
still write `pc+4` through the existing ALU-result path, and misaligned
branch/jump target traps still suppress redirects and clear register writes.

CoreMark cycles should be identical because every legal aligned direct JAL
that reaches EX was already predicted in IF, and every misaligned direct JAL
already follows the trap path with redirect suppression. The intended Pareto
win is a small LUT4 and/or Fmax improvement from removing the non-JALR jump
repair predicate and its fanout from the EX control cone. Do not change the
replay predictor, static branch direction rule, direct-JAL target rule,
JALR policy, pipeline depth, hazard interlocks, divider protocol, pending
store slot, memory traps, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0 retirement order,
channel-1 tie-off, decoder illegal defaults, top-level IO, or timing-marker
behavior.

changes.description:
Remove the `jal_mispredict` signal and make `jump_redirect` assert only
for valid, non-misaligned JALR instructions. Keep branch misprediction
repair based on `branch_taken != in.predicted_taken`, preserve direct
JAL and JALR target calculation, preserve misaligned-target trap gating,
preserve JAL/JALR `pc+4` link-data routing, and leave divider,
forwarding, EX/MEM valid/stall behavior, RVFI-visible `pc_next`, and all
module ports unchanged.

Update only prediction metadata comments if needed to clarify that
`predicted_taken` is still produced by IF for branches and direct JALs,
while EX consumes it only for conditional branch repair. Do not change
bundle fields, control encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field
widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

title: Shared Branch Compare Flags
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The accept rule is strict Pareto dominance, so this micro_opt slot
should target a local free win: keep CoreMark cycles unchanged while
reducing LUT4 or improving the EX-stage control path.

Recent experiments show that larger structural cleanups and reset-elision
attempts are placement-sensitive. The remaining promising micro-level
target is the branch comparator cone in `rtl/ex_stage.sv`. The current
`case` expression spells BEQ, BNE, BLT, BGE, BLTU, and BGEU as separate
relational operators. Synthesis may not reliably share the inverse pairs,
so BGE can become a second signed comparator instead of the inverse of BLT,
BGEU can become a second unsigned comparator instead of the inverse of
BLTU, and BNE can duplicate the equality tree used for BEQ.

Conditional branches are common in CoreMark's hot loops and sit in the same
EX cone that drives redirect repair and `pc_next`. Making the branch flags
explicit is narrower than changing the predictor, replay table, ALU
arithmetic, store slot, divider, pipeline payloads, or RVFI retirement. It
should preserve the instruction stream and cycle count while giving the
FPGA mapper a simpler compare/mux shape.
hypothesis:
In `rtl/ex_stage.sv`, replace the per-branch relational expressions inside
the branch-condition case with explicitly shared compare flags:

`cmp_eq = (rs1 == rs2)`;

`cmp_lts = ($signed(rs1) < $signed(rs2))`;

`cmp_ltu = (rs1 < rs2)`.

Then derive the six branch conditions from those flags: BEQ uses `cmp_eq`,
BNE uses `!cmp_eq`, BLT uses `cmp_lts`, BGE uses `!cmp_lts`, BLTU uses
`cmp_ltu`, and BGEU uses `!cmp_ltu`. Keep the existing `branch_taken =
in.ctrl.is_branch && branch_cond` gating, branch target calculation,
outcome-only prediction repair, direct-JAL/JALR handling, misaligned target
trap behavior, divider interlock, operand forwarding, EX/MEM register
semantics, and RVFI-visible `pc_next` behavior unchanged.

This should not alter CoreMark iterations/cycle because no pipeline state,
PC selection policy, replay behavior, stall rule, memory protocol, writeback
rule, or retirement rule changes. The intended Pareto win is a small LUT4
reduction and/or median Fmax improvement from replacing duplicate branch
compare operators with one equality tree, one signed less-than comparator,
and one unsigned less-than comparator. Top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports,
channel-0 single retirement, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`,
decoder illegal defaults, memory traps, pending-store behavior, divider
protocol, and CoreMark timing markers remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Add local `cmp_eq`, `cmp_lts`, and `cmp_ltu` wires in the branch resolve
section and derive BEQ/BNE/BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU from those shared flags.
Preserve the existing branch condition encoding, `branch_taken` gating,
branch and jump target arithmetic, prediction repair, misaligned-target
trap gating, JAL/JALR return-data routing, divider launch/completion,
operand forwarding, EX/MEM valid/stall behavior, and all RVFI-visible
payload semantics.

- Replaced the BEQ/BNE/BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU case arms with direct or inverted uses of those flags.

title: MEM/WB Final Writeback Rail
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500 target.
The structural slot should therefore look for a free win: preserve the
instruction stream and the already-good area result while improving Fmax or
trimming logic on a live pipeline boundary.

The MEM/WB bundle still carries two separate 32-bit result rails:
`alu_result` for ALU/JAL/JALR results and `read_data` for sign/zero-extended
loads. The only downstream consumers are the WB-stage load-versus-ALU mux,
MEM/WB forwarding through `wb_w_data`, the register-file write port, and
RVFI `rd_wdata`. All of those consumers need the same final architectural
writeback value, not both preselected alternatives.

Collapsing MEM/WB to a single final writeback-data rail is narrower than the
prior broad stage-local control split and does not touch the store slot,
replay frontend, branch repair, divider, or register file. It removes one
consumed 32-bit MEM/WB payload rail and moves the load-result select before
the register, shortening the MEM/WB-to-EX forwarding and MEM/WB-to-regfile
write-data cone without changing any cycle-level behavior.
hypothesis:
Replace `mem_wb_t.alu_result` and `mem_wb_t.read_data` with one registered
final result field, for example `rd_wdata`. In MEM, compute that field as the
existing sign/zero-extended `load_data` when `in.ctrl.mem_to_reg` is true,
otherwise as the existing `in.alu_result`. The MEM/WB hold behavior must
remain exactly as today: during `hold_wb`, clear only `valid` and retain the
previous `rd_wdata`, `rd`, and control payload so MEM/WB forwarding during a
dmem wait still sees the held producer.

Update WB so `w_data` is driven directly from the registered final result
field instead of from a load-versus-ALU mux. Keep `w_en` gated by
`in.ctrl.reg_write && in.valid`, and keep x0 suppression in the register
file unchanged. Core-level forwarding and RVFI can continue to use the
existing `wb_w_data` wire, so top-level IO and RVFI field wiring should not
need semantic changes.

This is a structural payload projection, not a control split or scheduling
change. Do not alter branch/JAL/JALR prediction or repair, replay-table
behavior, load-use interlocks, forwarding priority, pending-store behavior,
divider protocol, register-file behavior, memory masks/data, RVFI NRET=2
ports, channel-0-only single retirement, channel-1 tie-off, strict
`rvfi_order`, decoder illegal-default discipline, top-level IO, or CoreMark
timing markers. CoreMark cycles should remain unchanged; the intended Pareto
win is equal or better fitness from a shorter WB/forwarding cone and no LUT4
regression, with a likely FF reduction from deleting one redundant 32-bit
MEM/WB rail.

changes.description:

ALU opcode constants, decoder legality assumptions, top-level IO
assumptions, and the NRET=2 contract.

Preselect the final MEM/WB writeback value when updating the MEM/WB
register: use `load_data` for load-result instructions and
`in.alu_result` otherwise. Keep load extraction, byte/halfword masks,
misalignment traps, dmem request gating, store-slot enqueue/wait
behavior, MEM/WB valid-clear hold semantics, RVFI memory reporting, and
all module ports unchanged.

Remove the load-versus-ALU result mux from WB. Drive `w_data` directly
from the registered final MEM/WB writeback field while preserving the
current write enable condition, destination register output, regfile x0
behavior, and RVFI `rd_wdata` semantics through the existing top-level
`wb_w_data` path.

Keep the existing `wb_w_data` forwarding and RVFI wiring, but update any
comments or type references affected by the renamed MEM/WB final result
field. Preserve the `core` port list, channel-0 retirement boundary,
channel-1 tie-off, RVFI order counter, store-slot dmem arbitration,

title: Replay Candidate Bank Reuse
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The accept rule is strict Pareto dominance, so this predictor slot
should look for a free win: preserve the replay hit behavior and CoreMark
cycle count while reducing predictor-local compare logic or improving IF
placement.

The experiment history makes the boundary clear. Removing the replay table
spends too many iStall cycles, while compressed tags, MRU-free replacement,
lockstep candidate guards, branch-only metadata, and direct-JAL repair
pruning were placement-noisy or regressed Fmax. The two-bank registered
replay predictor itself is still the valuable predictor structure.

One part of that predictor is redundant: live fills recompute two current-PC
bank tag matches to decide whether to refresh bank 0, refresh bank 1, or
fill the non-MRU bank. For the same current PC, the registered replay
candidate was produced by the previous cycle's lookahead lookup using those
same two bank comparisons, and the existing full candidate-PC guard already
proves whether that candidate corresponds to the current fetch address.
Carrying one bank bit with the registered candidate should remove duplicate
fill-side tag compares without changing the current-cycle replay-hit path.
hypothesis:
Add a one-bit replay candidate bank field in `rtl/if_stage.sv`. During
lookahead generation for `replay_lookup_pc`, register which bank supplied
the candidate instruction, with the same bank-0 preference already used
when both banks match. During the existing fill-bypass case, record the bank
selected for the live fill.

Replace the live-fill `replay_fill_bank0_match` and
`replay_fill_bank1_match` recomparisons with candidate-bank reuse. On a live
`io_imemReady` fill, if `replay_current_hit` is true, refresh the recorded
candidate bank and mark that bank MRU. If the guarded candidate is not a hit
for the current PC, treat the fill as the existing miss case and allocate
the non-MRU bank, then mark the filled bank MRU.

This keeps lookup fully two-way associative: both bank tags are still
compared for the lookahead PC, live imem data still has priority over replay
data, the candidate PC equality guard still protects current-PC replay, and
replay misses still hold the PC and inject the existing IF bubble. Static
backward-branch prediction, aligned direct-JAL prediction, JALR EX
resolution, redirect priority, fill bypass, `fetch_ready`, and PC update
semantics remain unchanged.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should be unchanged because the replay table
contents, hit policy, replacement outcome for normal hit/miss cases, and
instruction stream are intended to be identical. The Pareto win is lower
LUT4 and/or higher Fmax from deleting two 23-bit fill-side tag compare trees
and their priority logic while adding only one valid-guarded candidate bank
bit. Top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0-only retirement, channel-1
tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder legality, memory traps, pending-store
behavior, divider protocol, and CoreMark marker behavior remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Add a one-bit registered replay-candidate bank field alongside the
candidate valid, PC, and instruction fields. Populate it from the
selected bank during the two-way lookahead lookup, and from the selected
fill bank during the same-entry fill-bypass path. Replace the live-fill
current-PC bank tag comparisons with guarded candidate-bank reuse:
refresh `replay_cand_bank_q` when `replay_current_hit` is true,
otherwise fill `~replay_mru[replay_fill_idx]`. Preserve both replay
banks, full high-PC tags, bank valid bits, instruction payload arrays,
MRU update semantics, fill bypass, live-imem priority, candidate PC
equality guard, static branch/direct-JAL prediction, JALR-unpredicted
behavior, redirect priority, PC update semantics, and `fetch_ready`.

Update only IF/ID replay predictor comments to document that the
registered lookahead candidate carries the bank that produced it, so
live fills can refresh a matching bank without recomparing the current
PC against both bank tags. Do not change bundle fields, control
encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO
assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changed

- Added `replay_cand_bank_q` in `rtl/if_stage.sv` alongside the registered replay candidate valid/PC/instruction fields.
- Added `replay_lookup_bank` from the existing two-bank lookahead lookup, preserving bank-0 preference when both banks hit.
- Removed the live-fill current-PC bank tag recomparisons. A live fill now refreshes `replay_cand_bank_q` when `replay_current_hit` is true; otherwise it allocates `~replay_mru[replay_fill_idx]`.
- Preserved fill bypass by recording the selected live-fill bank as the registered candidate bank when the fill and lookup refer to the same replay entry.
- Updated IF replay predictor comments in `rtl/if_stage.sv` and `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to document the candidate-bank reuse.

- The direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` invocation could not complete in this sandbox because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a checkout outside the worktree writable roots, and the script's staging cleanup hit `Operation not permitted` before any checks were generated.

title: Consumer-Aware Load-Use Interlock
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The accept rule is strict Pareto dominance, so this micro_opt slot
should target a free win on the performance axis without perturbing the
replay predictor, store buffer, divider, or RVFI contract.

The remaining single-cycle pipeline bubble that is most likely to be
avoidable is the load-use interlock. The current hazard unit treats both
raw instruction fields `instr[19:15]` and `instr[24:20]` as EX-stage source
operands for every following instruction. That is conservative, but it
inserts bubbles when a load is followed by an I-type/LOAD/JALR instruction
whose immediate bits happen to match the load destination, or by LUI/AUIPC
or direct JAL encodings that do not consume those fields at all.

There is also one common real dependency that does not need an EX-stage
bubble: load-to-store-data. A store needs rs1 in EX to form the address, but
rs2 store data is not consumed by the external memory port until MEM. The
load result from the immediately older instruction is available in MEM/WB by
then. The current interlock still stalls that case because it treats store
rs2 like an EX operand.

Tightening the interlock around the cycle in which each operand is actually
needed should reduce CoreMark cycles while adding only small decode and
MEM-stage mux logic. Unlike previous area-only cleanups, this directly
attacks the CoreMark fitness numerator through IPC and leaves the already
successful Fmax-critical frontend structures unchanged.
hypothesis:
Make the load-use hazard consumer-aware. The hazard unit should classify the
IF/ID instruction with a small opcode-only source-use predecode:

- rs1 is an EX-stage source for OP, OP-IMM, LOAD, STORE, BRANCH, and JALR.
- rs2 is an EX-stage source for OP and BRANCH.
- STORE rs2 is not an EX-stage source; it is store data consumed in MEM.
- LUI, AUIPC, direct JAL, FENCE, and EBREAK do not need a load-use stall on
  either raw register field.
- unknown or reserved opcode classes may conservatively keep the old stall
  behavior so illegal-instruction RVFI source reporting is not weakened.

Then change the load-use predicate so a load in ID/EX stalls the following
instruction only when the load destination matches an operand needed in EX,
or when the store address base rs1 matches. A following store whose only
dependency is rs2 store data may advance without the bubble.

To make that store-data exception correct, add a late store-data forwarding
path in MEM. When the MEM-stage instruction is a store and the held MEM/WB
producer writes the same nonzero register as the store's rs2, use the
existing `wb_w_data` value as the store data before byte/halfword
replication. Use that same late-forwarded value for the live dmem write
payload, pending-store enqueue payload, RVFI `mem_wdata`, and the retiring
store's RVFI `rs2_rdata`. The forwarding enable should intentionally follow
the existing MEM/WB forwarding convention and look at the retained
`mem_wb_w.ctrl.reg_write` plus `rd`, not `mem_wb_w.valid`, so it still works
across dmem wait cycles where MEM/WB valid is cleared but the producer data
is retained.

CoreMark cycles should improve by removing false load-use bubbles and
immediate load-to-store-data bubbles. Fmax and LUT4 should stay essentially
flat because the change is local: a small opcode predecode in the existing
hazard path and one 32-bit store-data mux in MEM. It does not change the
instruction fetch sequence, branch/JAL/JALR prediction or repair, replay
table behavior, divider protocol, pending-store scheduling, memory ordering,
writeback timing, top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0 retirement
ordering, channel-1 tie-off, decoder illegal-default discipline, or
CoreMark timing markers.

changes.description:
Replace the raw `id_ex_rd == if_id_rs1 || id_ex_rd == if_id_rs2`
load-use test with an opcode-aware consumer test. Add a small IF/ID
opcode input or derive it from the existing IF/ID instruction bits at
the top level. Stall when the load destination matches an operand needed
in EX: rs1 for OP/OP-IMM/LOAD/STORE/BRANCH/JALR and rs2 for OP/BRANCH.
Do not stall solely for STORE rs2, because store data will be forwarded
in MEM. Keep unknown opcode handling conservative, and preserve all
existing redirect, imem stall, mem_wait, ex_long_busy, EX/MEM hold, and
MEM/WB valid-clear behavior.

Add a late store-data forwarding input path from MEM/WB. For a valid
store in MEM, if the retained MEM/WB producer has `reg_write`, a nonzero
`rd`, and `rd == in.rs2_addr`, select `wb_w_data` as the raw store data;
otherwise use the existing EX/MEM `write_data`. Feed the selected raw
value into the existing byte/halfword/word replication, live dmem write
data, pending-store enqueue data, MEM/WB `mem_wdata`, and the store's
RVFI-visible `rs2_val`. Preserve load extraction, memory masks,
misalignment traps, `mem_access` gating, store-slot enqueue/wait
behavior, MEM/WB hold semantics, and all module ports other than the new
local forwarding inputs.

Wire the hazard unit's IF/ID opcode/source-use input from
`if_id_w.instr[6:0]` or equivalent local predecode. Wire the MEM-stage
late store-data forwarding inputs from the existing MEM/WB retirement
register and WB data mux: `mem_wb_w.rd`, `mem_wb_w.ctrl.reg_write`, and
`wb_w_data`. Preserve the core module IO, IF replay wiring, dmem
arbitration and pending-store drain priority, RVFI channel layout,
channel-0 order counter, channel-1 tie-off, and all existing forwarding
paths into EX.

Update only comments if useful to document that store rs2 data may be
corrected by a late MEM-stage forward while EX-stage source hazards are
limited to operands actually consumed in EX. Do not change bundle
fields, ALU encodings, decoder legality defaults, RVFI field widths,
top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Consumer-Aware Load-Use Interlock

## What changed

- `rtl/hazard_unit.sv` now predecodes the IF/ID opcode and only stalls a load-use pair when the younger instruction needs the matching register in EX.
- Store `rs2` is no longer treated as an EX-stage source, so load-to-store-data can advance without the immediate bubble when the store address base is independent.
- `rtl/mem_stage.sv` now accepts retained MEM/WB forwarding inputs and uses `wb_fwd_data` for store data when `wb_fwd_reg_write && wb_fwd_rd != 0 && wb_fwd_rd == in.rs2_addr`.
- The late-forwarded store data feeds byte/halfword replication, live dmem write data, pending-store enqueue data, MEM/WB `mem_wdata`, and RVFI `rs2_val`.
- MEM-stage RVFI source snapshots for both `rs1` and `rs2` also use the same retained MEM/WB correction when their raw register field matches the retained producer.
- `rtl/forward_unit.sv` now skips EX/MEM load-result forwarding for raw fields that are not consumed in EX. This prevents a non-consuming raw source from capturing a load address, especially when the load later traps and does not write back.
- `rtl/core.sv` wires the new hazard, MEM-stage, and forwarding inputs. Top-level IO is unchanged.
- `rtl/core_pkg.sv` has a comment update documenting that store `rs2` can be corrected in MEM.

## Deviations

- I added an exact EBREAK input to the hazard predecode so EBREAK can be source-free while other SYSTEM encodings remain conservative.
- I extended the MEM-stage late forward beyond store `rs2` to RVFI source snapshots. The existing cosim/formal contract reports raw instruction source fields, so removing false load-use stalls requires keeping those raw source values coherent even when the fields are not architectural EX operands.
- I also made forwarding consumer-aware for non-EX raw fields. Without this, a JAL/LUI/AUIPC-style raw field could incorrectly pick up an EX/MEM load address before the load is resolved.

## Concerns

- The added logic is local, but there is now a small duplicate opcode predecode in `hazard_unit.sv` and `forward_unit.sv`.
- No replay predictor, store-slot policy, divider protocol, top-level IO, or RVFI channel layout was changed.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh` from the worktree was blocked before proof by sandbox permissions because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared directory outside the writable roots.
- Equivalent local formal was run by staging `rtl/`, `formal/run_all.sh`, wrapper/config files, and a writable copy of `formal/riscv-formal` under `/tmp/codex-formal-run-49483`: pass, `105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Use-Aware Load-Use Interlock
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The structural slot therefore needs a free win: improve CoreMark
throughput without giving back the tight area result or weakening the
RVFI/formal contract.

The remaining structural opportunity is in the load-use interlock. Today
`hazard_unit.sv` conservatively stalls whenever a load in ID/EX writes the
same register number as either raw source field in the following IF/ID
instruction. That is correct but over-conservative: for OP-IMM, LOAD, JALR,
LUI, AUIPC, JAL, FENCE, and EBREAK, instruction bits [24:20] are not an
architectural rs2 source, and for LUI/AUIPC/JAL/FENCE/EBREAK even bits
[19:15] are not consumed by the executing instruction. If those immediate
bits happen to equal the preceding load's rd, the core injects a bubble
even though no dependent value is needed.

This is narrower than adding a bypass, changing the replay frontend,
touching the store buffer, or restructuring pipeline payloads. It targets
only false load-use bubbles, so true RAW hazards still get the existing
one-cycle interlock and all forwarding semantics remain unchanged.
hypothesis:
Make the load-use detector source-use aware. Pass the IF/ID instruction
word into `hazard_unit.sv`, or equivalently pass decode-light `uses_rs1` and
`uses_rs2` bits, and qualify the existing rd comparisons with those use
predicates.

The source-use classification should be conservative and opcode-local:
R-type OP/M uses rs1 and rs2; STORE and BRANCH use rs1 and rs2; OP-IMM,
LOAD, and JALR use only rs1; LUI, AUIPC, JAL, FENCE, and the legal EBREAK
path use neither source. Unknown, reserved, or otherwise illegal encodings
should default to the current conservative behavior by treating both raw
fields as potentially used, so decoder illegal-default discipline and trap
reporting are not made dependent on this optimization.

The final load-use predicate should remain:
`load in ID/EX && rd != x0 && ((uses_rs1 && rd == rs1) || (uses_rs2 && rd == rs2))`.
Do not change the one-cycle stall for real load consumers, MEM wait holds,
EX long-operation holds, redirect flushing, IF replay behavior, register
file reads, forwarding priority, pending-store behavior, divider protocol,
RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0-only retirement, channel-1 tie-off, strict
`rvfi_order`, or top-level IO.

CoreMark cycles should drop slightly by removing false bubbles after loads,
especially in immediate-heavy compiled code. The added opcode-local source
classifier is small enough that LUT4 should stay flat or improve through
reduced hazard-control activity and no new pipeline state. The intended
phase-2 Pareto win is higher CoreMark iter/s with LUT4 no worse than the
current 3449 result.

changes.description:
Replace the raw two-source load-use comparison with a source-use-aware
predicate. Add decode-light logic from the IF/ID instruction opcode to
compute `uses_rs1` and `uses_rs2`, qualify the existing rd==rs1/rs2
comparisons with those bits, and default unknown or illegal-looking
opcodes to conservative both-source behavior. Preserve all existing
stall/flush priorities for redirect, imem stalls, MEM waits, and
long EX operations.

Update the hazard-unit instantiation to provide the IF/ID instruction
word, or the equivalent source-use sideband bits, while preserving the
core module IO, IF replay wiring, pipeline stage topology, MEM wait
handling, RVFI order counter, channel-0 retirement boundary, and
channel-1 tie-off.

Update comments only if needed to document that IF/ID raw rs fields are
still carried for decode/RVFI, while the load-use interlock now ignores
source fields that the next instruction does not architecturally
consume. Do not change bundle fields, control encodings, RVFI field
widths, decoder illegal defaults, or the NRET=2 contract.

title: Synchronous BRAM Replay Predictor
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The accept rule is strict Pareto dominance, so this predictor slot
should look for a free win: keep the CoreMark instruction stream and replay
hit behavior unchanged while reducing predictor LUT use or improving IF
timing.

The experiment history is clear that the two-bank instruction replay
predictor is the predictor feature worth preserving. Removing it exposed
too much random iStall penalty, while replacement-policy tweaks, compressed
tags, candidate-guard changes, and direct-JAL metadata pruning were
placement-noisy or regressed Fmax. The remaining high-value angle is not a
new predictor policy; it is changing the replay table's storage shape so
the same predictor can map out of LUT fabric.

Today each replay bank is an asynchronously read array of 128 instruction
words plus 128 high-PC tags. That is roughly fourteen kilobits of
predictor-local payload with combinational read and tag-compare logic
feeding the registered lookahead candidate. Because the design already uses
the table only through a one-cycle lookahead candidate, it does not require
asynchronous RAM semantics. A synchronous registered-read replay table can
provide the same candidate phase while giving Yosys/nextpnr a much better
chance to infer Gowin block RAM instead of LUTRAM.
hypothesis:
Convert the two replay banks in `rtl/if_stage.sv` from asynchronous
tag/instruction arrays to synchronous registered-read memories, preferably
with each bank storing a packed `{tag_hi, instr}` payload and keeping the
resettable valid bits separate. The read address remains the existing
`replay_lookup_idx`, selected from redirect target, advancing `next_pc`, or
held `pc`. The registered read output, together with the delayed
`replay_lookup_pc`, becomes the replay candidate for the following fetch
cycle, exactly matching the current lookahead timing.

Preserve the current replay semantics. Live imem data still wins over
replay data for the current instruction. The full candidate-PC guard still
proves that a replay candidate belongs to the current fetch PC. Both banks
are still compared for the lookahead PC using full `pc[31:9]` tags. The
same-entry fill bypass still supplies `io_imemData` as the next registered
candidate when the current live fill is also the lookahead entry, so the
synchronous memory read latency does not introduce a new replay miss. MRU
replacement, bank valid resets, static backward-branch prediction, aligned
direct-JAL prediction, JALR EX resolution, redirect priority, `fetch_ready`,
and PC update semantics remain unchanged.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should be identical because this is a storage
implementation change for the existing registered replay predictor, not a
change to its hit policy or next-PC policy. The intended Pareto win is a
lower LUT4 count and possibly higher median Fmax by moving replay payload
storage and the lookup path into synchronous block-RAM-friendly logic. The
top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0-only retirement, channel-1
tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder legality, memory traps, pending-store
behavior, divider protocol, and CoreMark marker behavior remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Reshape the two-bank replay table into synchronous registered-read
memories. Pack each bank's resetless high-PC tag and instruction word
into a block-RAM-friendly payload array, keep bank valid bits as
resettable state, and register the read outputs for the existing
lookahead candidate phase. Preserve full tag comparison, the
`replay_cand_pc_q == pc` guard, live-imem priority, same-entry fill
bypass, MRU replacement, bank valid reset behavior, static branch and
direct-JAL prediction, JALR-unpredicted behavior, redirect priority,
`fetch_ready`, and all PC update semantics.

Update only IF/ID replay predictor comments to document that the replay
payload banks are synchronous registered-read predictor memories while
prediction metadata and bundle fields are unchanged. Do not change
bundle widths, control encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field
widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes

- Converted the two IF replay predictor payload banks in `rtl/if_stage.sv` from separate asynchronous tag and instruction arrays into packed `{tag_hi, instr}` synchronous registered-read memories.
- Kept replay valid bits and MRU replacement state as resettable state outside the payload memories.
- Preserved the registered lookahead candidate phase by comparing the registered read payload tags against the delayed lookup PC, with the existing full `replay_cand_pc_q == pc` current-PC guard.
- Preserved live-imem priority and the same-entry fill bypass by carrying a one-cycle bypass candidate when the live fill is also the lookahead entry.
- Added one-cycle last-write tag metadata for fill-bank selection so a read/write collision does not make the replacement logic treat a just-overwritten stale read payload as the current table contents.
- Updated the IF/ID replay predictor comment in `rtl/core_pkg.sv`; no bundle fields, widths, encodings, RVFI ports, decoder behavior, or top-level IO were changed.

## Deviations

- The implementation uses a small last-write helper for fill-bank matching. This is not new prediction policy; it compensates for the synchronous payload read when the current fill index just experienced a write/read collision.
- The standard `bash formal/run_all.sh` could not stage files in this sandbox because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to `/Users/bonetto/bonetto/auto-arch-tournament/formal/riscv-formal`, outside the writable worktree. I ran the same staging/check flow from a copied `/tmp` riscv-formal tree instead.

## Concerns

- The payload memories are annotated with `ram_style = "block"` and `syn_ramstyle = "block_ram"` to encourage BRAM inference, but final Gowin mapping still depends on the synthesis flow's memory inference.
- The registered-read payload relies on read-before-write behavior for replay candidates in same-index conflict cases; the explicit same-entry fill bypass covers the intended live-fill hit case.

## Local Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: blocked before checks by sandbox permission errors while removing staged files through the external `formal/riscv-formal` symlink.
- Equivalent `/tmp` formal staging using the same `formal/checks.cfg`, `formal/wrapper.sv`, current `rtl/*.sv`, and riscv-formal checks: pass, `105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Factored Decoder Encodings
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The accept rule is strict Pareto dominance, so this micro_opt slot
should target a small no-cycle-change logic reduction that improves LUT4 or
Fmax without disturbing the replay predictor, store slot, divider, or RVFI
contract.

Several recent timing-oriented cleanups were placement-sensitive because
they touched IF, EX, or MEM datapaths. The decoder is a safer remaining
micro-optimization target: it is purely combinational, has no architectural
state, and already encodes some control fields in a way that makes large
case statements redundant.

Two exact encodings are currently left for synthesis to rediscover. First,
every RV32M R-type funct3 maps directly onto the low three bits of the
existing ALU_M extension op encoding: `ALU_MUL` through `ALU_REMU` are
`{2'b10, funct3}`. Second, the legal load/store memory width field is
exactly `funct3[1:0]`: LB/LBU both map to byte, LH/LHU both map to halfword,
and LW/SW map to word. Replacing enumerated decode cases with these direct
encodings should trim decoder muxing and control fanout while leaving the
instruction stream and all pipeline timing unchanged.
hypothesis:
Factor the decoder around encodings already present in the ISA and
`core_pkg.sv`.

In the R-type OP decode, keep the existing legality rule for
`funct7 == 7'b0000001`, but drive the ALU operation for all RV32M
instructions as `{2'b10, funct3}` instead of enumerating all eight M
operations in the wide `{funct7, funct3}` case. The ALU constants are already
chosen so this is bit-identical for MUL, MULH, MULHSU, MULHU, DIV, DIVU,
REM, and REMU. Keep the funct7=0 base-RV32I case and the funct7=0x20
SUB/SRA case legal behavior unchanged.

In the LOAD and STORE decode arms, replace the width-selection case
statements with direct assignment from `funct3[1:0]` after the existing
valid-funct3 checks pass. For loads, this preserves byte width for LB/LBU,
halfword width for LH/LHU, and word width for LW; `mem_sext = ~funct3[2]`
remains unchanged. For stores, SB/SH/SW likewise map directly to 0/1/2.

This is a decoder strength reduction only. It must not change the default
illegal discipline, SYSTEM/EBREAK policy, FENCE behavior, branch/JAL/JALR
prediction or repair, load-use interlock, forwarding, pending-store
behavior, memory masks or traps, divider protocol, writeback timing,
top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0-only retirement, channel-1
tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, or CoreMark timing markers. CoreMark cycles
should be identical; the intended Pareto win is a small LUT4 reduction and
possibly a slight Fmax improvement from a simpler decode cone.

changes.description:
Refactor only the combinational decode logic. For RV32M R-type
instructions with `funct7 == 7'b0000001`, assign `alu_op` directly as
`{2'b10, funct3}` instead of selecting each multiply/divide/remainder
operation in the wide case. For LOAD and STORE instructions, assign
`mem_width = funct3[1:0]` after the existing valid-funct3 checks rather
than enumerating byte/halfword/word cases. Preserve every legality
check, default assignment, decoder port, control signal, ALU op value,
memory sign-extension rule, and EBREAK/FENCE policy exactly.

Update only comments if useful to document that the RV32M ALU op
encoding intentionally matches `{2'b10, funct3}` and that memory widths
follow `funct3[1:0]` for legal load/store encodings. Do not change any
constants, bundle fields, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions,
or the NRET=2 contract.

- Kept the existing RV32I `funct7 == 7'b0000000` and SUB/SRA

title: Derive RVFI Source Addresses From Instruction
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The structural slot should therefore look for a free win: remove
real pipeline payload without changing the instruction stream, stall policy,
memory ordering, or RVFI contract.

The pipeline carries `rs1_addr` and `rs2_addr` through ID/EX, EX/MEM, and
MEM/WB even though the full 32-bit instruction word is already carried
through the same stages. Those address fields are always just
`instr[19:15]` and `instr[24:20]`. The ID/EX copies feed EX forwarding, and
the MEM/WB copies feed RVFI, but neither consumer needs separately
registered address rails when the instruction bits are already present and
stable in the same pipeline register.

Prior structural payload experiments removed data rails or control fields
that either were already optimized away or perturbed placement-sensitive
datapaths. This hypothesis is narrower: keep the RVFI source data value
rails, store-data rail, branch metadata, control payload, forwarding
priority, and all scheduling behavior unchanged. Remove only duplicated
source-register address bits and recover them by slicing the stage-local
instruction word at the point of use.
hypothesis:
Delete `rs1_addr` and `rs2_addr` from the ID/EX, EX/MEM, and MEM/WB pipeline
bundles. ID should continue to drive the register-file read ports directly
from the IF/ID instruction bits, but it should no longer latch separate
source-address fields into ID/EX. EX forwarding should use
`id_ex_w.instr[19:15]` and `id_ex_w.instr[24:20]` as the source register
numbers instead of `id_ex_w.rs1_addr` and `id_ex_w.rs2_addr`.

EX should keep capturing the post-forwarded `rs1_val` and `rs2_val` value
rails exactly as today for branch comparison, store data, divider shadow
state, and RVFI source-data reporting. It should stop copying source-address
fields into EX/MEM. MEM should likewise stop copying source-address fields
into MEM/WB. At retirement, the top-level RVFI source address outputs should
be driven directly from `mem_wb_w.instr[19:15]` and `mem_wb_w.instr[24:20]`,
preserving the current raw-field reporting convention for legal and illegal
instructions.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should be identical because no PC selection,
decode legality, operand value, branch/JAL/JALR repair, load-use interlock,
forwarding priority, divider protocol, pending-store behavior, dmem
handshake, writeback rule, or retirement rule changes. The intended Pareto
win is lower LUT4 and FF/reset pressure from removing roughly thirty
duplicated pipeline register bits and their assignment muxing, with Fmax
flat or slightly improved because the replacement logic is only local
instruction bit slicing. Top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0-only
retirement, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, memory traps, and
CoreMark timing markers remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Remove `rs1_addr` and `rs2_addr` from `id_ex_t`, `ex_mem_t`, and
`mem_wb_t`. Update comments to document that source register addresses
are derived from the carried instruction word, while `rs1_val` and
`rs2_val` remain the post-forwarded RVFI source-data value rails. Do not
change ALU encodings, control fields, memory payload fields, RVFI field
widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

Keep driving the register-file read addresses from `in.instr[19:15]` and
`in.instr[24:20]`, but stop latching those addresses into the ID/EX
bundle. Preserve decode, immediate generation, register-file read data
capture, prediction metadata, stall/flush behavior, and valid
propagation.

Stop assigning source-address fields into EX/MEM on both the normal
execute path and the divide-complete path. Preserve the existing
post-forwarded `rs1` and `rs2` value capture, divider shadow operands,
branch comparisons, JAL/JALR target handling, misaligned-target trap
gating, EX/MEM valid/stall behavior, and store-data path.

Stop copying source-address fields into MEM/WB. Keep `rs1_val` and
`rs2_val` data values, load extraction, byte/halfword masks,
misalignment traps, dmem request gating, store-slot enqueue/wait
behavior, MEM/WB hold semantics, and RVFI memory reporting unchanged.

Update the EX forwarding inputs to use `id_ex_w.instr[19:15]` and
`id_ex_w.instr[24:20]`. Drive `io_rvfi_rs1_addr_0` and
`io_rvfi_rs2_addr_0` from `mem_wb_w.instr[19:15]` and
`mem_wb_w.instr[24:20]`. Preserve the core module IO, IF replay wiring,
dmem arbitration, RVFI order counter, channel-0 retirement boundary, and
channel-1 tie-off.

## What changed

- Removed `rs1_addr` and `rs2_addr` from the `id_ex_t`, `ex_mem_t`, and `mem_wb_t` pipeline bundles in `rtl/core_pkg.sv`.
- Left the ID-stage register-file read ports driven directly from `in.instr[19:15]` and `in.instr[24:20]`, but stopped latching those fields into ID/EX.

title: Fetch-Kill-Free Static Predictor Enable
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 517.86
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3449 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The predictor slot therefore needs a free win: improve Fmax or trim
predictor-local logic without changing CoreMark cycles, replay hit behavior,
or any architectural contract.

The last successful predictor change was a very small timing cleanup in the
IF static prediction cone: removing generic stall from the prediction enable.
The same argument still applies to the remaining reset/flush/redirect kill
gating. The static predictor result is not architecturally consumed on killed
IF cycles. Redirect already has priority in the next-PC mux and PC register,
reset already forces the PC to reset, and flush cycles emit an invalid NOP
into ID. When a replay miss causes an imem-stall flush, the PC is held and
the replay lookahead explicitly selects the held PC, so any branch/JAL
prediction computed from the placeholder instruction is ignored.

That makes `predict_enable = !fetch_kill` redundant control fanout in the
branch and direct-JAL prediction predicates. Removing it is narrower than
changing the replay table, branch direction policy, JALR behavior, or EX
repair logic, and it follows the only recent predictor pattern that improved
the selected design.
hypothesis:
Remove `predict_enable` from the IF-stage static branch/direct-JAL
prediction predicates. Conditional branches should still predict taken only
when the fetched instruction is a legal B-type branch, the immediate is
negative, and the computed target is word-aligned. Direct JALs should still
predict taken only when the fetched instruction is JAL and the computed
target is word-aligned. JALR remains unpredicted.

Keep `fetch_kill` exactly where it is architecturally meaningful: selecting
NOP for `out.instr`, clearing `out.valid`, and giving redirect/reset/flush
priority over ordinary fetch advancement. Keep the PC update rule unchanged:
reset wins, redirect overrides stalls, and otherwise the PC loads the
IF-computed `next_pc` only when `!stall`. Keep `replay_lookup_pc` unchanged
so stalled cycles still register a candidate for the held PC.

On killed cycles, `out.predicted_taken` may reflect the ignored instruction
word, but `out.valid` is zero and the ID/EX register either flushes or
captures an invalid payload. EX prediction repair continues to look only at
valid control-flow instructions. Therefore CoreMark iterations/cycle should
remain identical, while the IF prediction cone loses a small high-fanout
reset/flush/redirect gate. The intended Pareto win is equal IPC with slightly
higher median Fmax and no LUT4 regression.

Do not change the replay predictor, live-imem priority, fill bypass, MRU
replacement, branch direction rule, direct-JAL target rule, JALR policy,
misaligned-target trap handling, divider protocol, pending-store behavior,
memory traps, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0 retirement order, channel-1
tie-off, decoder illegal defaults, top-level IO, or CoreMark marker behavior.

changes.description:
Delete the `predict_enable` signal or stop using it in
`branch_predict_taken` and `jal_predict_taken`. Leave the B-type opcode
and funct3 legality checks, backward-branch rule, target-alignment
checks, direct-JAL prediction, JALR-unpredicted behavior, `next_pc`
priority, PC register update rule, replay lookup PC selection, replay
candidate guard, fill bypass, MRU replacement, live-imem priority, and
`fetch_ready` semantics unchanged. Keep `fetch_kill` for invalidating the
IF/ID payload.

Update only IF/ID prediction comments if useful to clarify that
`predicted_taken` is meaningful only when `valid` is high. Do not change
bundle fields, control encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field
widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

# Implementation Notes

title: X0-Through-Valid Register Reads
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 524.31
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3445 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The micro_opt slot should therefore look for a true free win:
preserve CoreMark cycles while shaving LUT4 or improving the Fmax-limiting
read/decode path.

The register file is the most attractive remaining local target. The
resetless valid-masked form is already the reason the design fits the LUT
target, but each read port still checks `rs?_addr == 0` before checking
same-cycle writeback and the per-register valid bit. Bit 0 of the valid mask
is already reset to zero and is never set, because writes to x0 are dropped.
That means the valid mask itself can enforce x0 reads as zero without a
separate per-port x0 compare and mux level.

This is deliberately narrower than the failed x0-elided register-file
variants: keep the 32-entry mirrored read banks, keep the 32-bit valid mask,
and do not remap architectural register numbers. Only remove the redundant
read-side x0 special case by making the existing nonzero-write predicate
guard same-cycle bypass and valid-bit updates.
hypothesis:
Refactor `rtl/reg_file.sv` so the read ports rely on `valid_q[0] == 0` for
x0 hardwiring. Add a shared write-fire predicate:

`w_fire = w_en && (w_addr != 5'b0)`.

Use `w_fire` for the sequential write to both read banks and for setting
`valid_q[w_addr]`. On each read port, select same-cycle writeback only when
`w_fire && w_addr == rs?_addr`; otherwise return the stored bank value only
when `valid_q[rs?_addr]` is set, and zero when it is clear. Since reset
clears all valid bits and `w_fire` can never set bit 0, reads of x0 remain
zero, including on a same-cycle instruction that writes x0.

This preserves every externally visible register-file behavior: x0 is
hardwired zero, nonzero same-cycle writeback is still write-first, valid
nonzero registers return stored data, and invalid nonzero registers read as
zero after reset. It does not change the module interface, storage geometry,
pipeline schedule, forwarding priority, load-use interlock, branch/JAL/JALR
behavior, memory ordering, divider protocol, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0
retirement order, channel-1 tie-off, decoder illegal defaults, or timing
markers.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should be identical. The intended Pareto win is a
small LUT4 reduction and/or Fmax improvement by deleting two read-port x0
equality checks and their priority muxing from the regfile-to-ID/EX path
while keeping the area-saving resetless valid-mask implementation.

changes.description:
Add a shared `w_fire = w_en && w_addr != 5'b0` predicate and use it for
writes, valid-bit updates, and same-cycle read bypass qualification.
Remove the explicit `rs1_addr == 5'b0` and `rs2_addr == 5'b0` read-side
priority cases; instead let reset-cleared, never-set `valid_q[0]` force
x0 reads to zero. Keep the two mirrored resetless read banks, 32-bit
valid mask, module ports, nonzero write behavior, write-first semantics
for nonzero destinations, and invalid-register read-as-zero behavior
unchanged.

title: Valid-Guarded Pipeline Reset Pruning
motivation:
The current selected RTL meets both phase-2 targets: 524.31 CoreMark
iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3445 LUT4 versus the 3500 target. The
structural slot therefore needs a free win: improve Fmax or area without
changing CoreMark cycles, the replay frontend, store buffering, divider
protocol, or the RVFI contract.

A large amount of pipeline state is still synchronously reset even though
most of those bits are payload values guarded by an adjacent valid bit and
by reset-safe control fields. The ID/EX, EX/MEM, and MEM/WB registers clear
full structs on reset and bubbles. That adds reset muxing and reset fanout
on PCs, operands, immediates, instruction words, memory data, and RVFI
metadata that are not architecturally observable unless the stage is valid.

Previous structural payload removals were too small or placement-noisy, and
resetless experiments on the divider/store slot touched cold or tiny state.
This hypothesis targets the larger structural reset pattern in the hot
pipeline registers while keeping every unguarded control bit reset-safe.
hypothesis:
Convert the inter-stage pipeline registers to valid-guarded reset style.
On reset and injected bubbles, explicitly clear only the bits that can
affect control, forwarding, retirement, or external enables when the stage is
invalid: at minimum `valid`, the carried control payload, destination `rd`,
and any small sideband that is consumed without a valid check. Leave the
wide data payload fields resetless, including PCs, instruction words,
operands, immediates, ALU/load data, memory data, and RVFI metadata.

In ID, a reset or flush should make `id_ex_t` non-valid and control-safe so
the hazard unit does not see a false load and EX does not launch a branch,
jump, memory operation, or divide from invalid payload. In EX, reset and
divide-busy bubbles should make `ex_mem_t` non-valid with `reg_write`,
memory controls, and `rd` cleared so forwarding and MEM access are disabled.
In MEM, reset should make `mem_wb_t` non-valid with writeback/trap control
and `rd` cleared; the existing `hold_wb` behavior must remain unchanged and
continue to retain the previous producer for MEM/WB forwarding during dmem
waits.

This is a reset-structure change, not a scheduling change. It must not alter
PC selection, branch/JAL/JALR prediction or repair, load-use interlocks,
forwarding priority, store-slot ordering, dmem handshake behavior, divider
latency, writeback timing, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0-only retirement,
channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder illegal defaults, memory
traps, or CoreMark timing markers.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical. The intended Pareto win
is a small LUT4 reduction and/or higher median Fmax from removing reset
muxes and reset fanout on several hundred guarded pipeline payload bits,
while preserving deterministic safe control after reset and bubbles.

changes.description:
Replace the full-struct reset/flush assignment for the ID/EX register
with explicit control-safe clearing. Reset and flush should clear
`reg_q.valid`, `reg_q.ctrl`, and `reg_q.rd` at minimum, while leaving
wide payload fields such as `pc`, `rs1_val`, `rs2_val`, `imm`, `instr`,
and prediction metadata resetless if they are only consumed when the
stage is valid. Preserve decode, immediate generation, register-file
read addresses, stall behavior, and valid propagation on normal captures.

Change EX/MEM reset and bubble handling so invalid bubbles clear only
the forwarding/control-visible fields: `valid`, `ctrl`, `rd`, and any
small sideband needed to keep invalid control deterministic. Leave wide
payload fields resetless where they are guarded by `valid` and cleared
control. Preserve divide launch/completion, EX/MEM hold on MEM wait,
branch and jump target handling, misaligned-target trap gating, operand
forwarding, JAL/JALR link-data routing, and all RVFI-visible semantics
for valid instructions.

Change MEM/WB reset handling so reset clears `valid`, `ctrl`, and `rd`
rather than zeroing the entire MEM/WB payload. Keep the existing
`hold_wb` semantics exactly: during a MEM wait, clear only `valid` and
retain the previous data/control fields for forwarding. Preserve load
extraction, byte/halfword masks, misalignment traps, dmem request
gating, pending-store enqueue/wait behavior, MEM/WB fields on valid
instructions, and RVFI memory reporting.

Update pipeline-bundle comments to document that wide payload members
may be resetless and are valid/control guarded, while `valid`, control
bits, and destination register fields remain reset-safe. Do not change
bundle field names or widths, ALU encodings, decoder legality defaults,
RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Updated `rtl/id_stage.sv` so ID/EX reset and flush clear only `rd`, `ctrl`, and `valid`. Wide payload fields (`pc`, operand values, immediate, instruction, and prediction metadata) are left resetless and invalid/control guarded.
- Updated `rtl/ex_stage.sv` so EX/MEM reset and divide-busy bubbles clear only `rd`, `ctrl`, and `valid`. Normal captures, divide completion capture, EX/MEM stall hold, branch repair, jump routing, and trap control propagation are unchanged.
- Updated `rtl/mem_stage.sv` so MEM/WB reset clears only `rd`, `ctrl`, and `valid`. The existing `hold_wb` behavior is unchanged: it clears `valid` only and retains the previous producer for MEM/WB forwarding during dmem waits.
- Updated `rtl/core_pkg.sv` comments to document that wide pipeline payload rails may be resetless behind valid/control guarding, while `valid`, `ctrl`, and `rd` remain reset-safe.

## Deviations

- No RTL behavior deviations from the hypothesis plan.
- The exact `bash formal/run_all.sh` invocation in the worktree could not stage files because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a protected location in this sandbox. I mirrored the edited RTL and formal runner into `/tmp/auto_arch_formal_r12s1` with a writable `cores` staging directory and ran the same script there.

## Concerns

- No known implementation concerns. Invalid stage payload values may now retain old or arbitrary data, but all control-visible consumers are guarded by cleared `valid`, `ctrl`, and `rd` fields. MEM/WB `hold_wb` still intentionally retains control/data for forwarding.

## Local verification
