title: Cold High-Half Multiply Unit
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 526.32
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3414 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The structural slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto free
win: keep the CoreMark instruction stream and cycle count unchanged while
reducing LUT pressure or improving median Fmax.

The ALU still places all RV32M multiply variants in the one-cycle EX
datapath. That means the hot CoreMark MUL path carries the synthesis and
routing cost of signed high-half products, even though normal C integer
multiplication only needs the low 32 bits. A previous single-product
correction rewrite was placement-sensitive because it kept the high-half
correction subtractors in the same combinational ALU cone. The more
structural version is to split cold high-half multiply operations out of the
one-cycle ALU path entirely.

DIV/REM already use this pattern successfully: cold multi-cycle arithmetic
occupies EX with `ex_long_busy`, emits bubbles while running, and retires
through the normal EX/MEM and MEM/WB path when complete. Applying the same
topology only to MULH, MULHSU, and MULHU should remove wide signed
multiplier logic from the hot path while leaving low-half MUL single-cycle
for CoreMark.
hypothesis:
Keep `ALU_MUL` as the only combinational multiply operation in `alu.sv`, and
compute only the low 32 product bits needed by RV32M MUL. Move MULH,
MULHSU, and MULHU into a cold multi-cycle high-half multiply unit launched
from EX, using the same long-operation interlock style as the existing
divider. The high-half unit may use an iterative shift/add implementation
over unsigned magnitudes plus final sign correction, or another small
sequential implementation, but it must produce exact RV32M results for all
signed, signed-unsigned, and unsigned high-half cases.

EX should treat MULH/MULHSU/MULHU as long operations: start the unit only
for a valid, legal instruction when EX/MEM is not stalled; hold IF/ID with
`ex_long_busy` while the unit is active; inject EX/MEM bubbles while the
operation is running; and emit one normal EX/MEM payload on completion with
the saved PC, instruction, forwarded source values, destination register,
`pc + 4`, `reg_write`, `mem_to_reg = 0`, no memory access, and the unit
result as `alu_result`. DIV/REM behavior and latency should remain
unchanged.

Under `RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`, the new high-half multiply path must still
produce the same ALTOPS formulas currently associated with MULH, MULHSU,
and MULHU, preferably with a short bounded latency so the existing formal
depth remains practical. The real hardware path must remain bit-exact and
continue to be covered by the existing M-extension cosim and ALU/unit tests.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain unchanged because the benchmark is
expected to use low-half MUL, not high-half multiply instructions. The
intended Pareto win is lower LUT4 and/or higher Fmax from removing signed
high-half multiplier hardware from the one-cycle ALU cone while preserving
the already-met LUT target. Top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0-only
retirement, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder legality,
branch/JAL/JALR repair, replay prediction, load-use interlocks,
pending-store behavior, memory traps, divider correctness, and CoreMark
marker behavior remain unchanged.

changes.description:
Restrict the one-cycle real multiply datapath to low-half `ALU_MUL`.
Remove the real signed-signed and signed-unsigned product expressions
from the hot ALU path, and make MULH/MULHSU/MULHU return a harmless
placeholder from `alu.sv` because EX will route those opcodes through
the cold high-half unit. Preserve all RV32I simple operations, the
final hot/cold result split, DIV/REM zero placeholders, module ports,
and the existing ALTOPS formulas needed for formal substitution.

Add a small sequential high-half multiply unit for MULH, MULHSU, and
MULHU. It should accept start/op/a/b, expose busy/done/result, reset
only the protocol state needed for safe idle behavior, and compute
exact RV32M high-half results in the real hardware path. Under
`RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS`, produce the matching ALTOPS high-half formulas
with bounded latency. Do not implement DIV/REM here; the existing
`div_unit.sv` remains authoritative for those operations.

Launch the new high-half multiply unit for valid, legal
MULH/MULHSU/MULHU instructions and fold it into the existing
long-operation protocol beside the divider. Save the minimal PC,
instruction, destination, and forwarded source-value metadata needed
to build the completed EX/MEM payload. Keep low-half MUL on the
existing one-cycle ALU path, keep DIV/REM behavior unchanged, preserve
branch and jump repair, misaligned-target trap gating, forwarding,
EX/MEM stall handling, JAL/JALR link routing, and all RVFI-visible
semantics.

Update comments only, if useful, to document that the ALU op encoding
now has a hot one-cycle MUL path and cold multi-cycle high-half
multiply path for MULH/MULHSU/MULHU. Do not change ALU constants,
bundle fields, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level
IO assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

title: Non-Speculative Return Address Predictor
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 526.32
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3414 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. This predictor slot therefore needs a free win: improve CoreMark
throughput without giving back the tight LUT result or weakening the RVFI
contract.

The experiment history says the existing two-bank instruction replay
predictor should be kept intact. Removing, retiming, compressing,
direct-mapping, or repacking that table generally lost more median Fmax or
replay hit behavior than it saved. The remaining predictor gap is control
flow that the static branch/direct-JAL predictor deliberately does not
cover: JALR returns. Direct JAL calls are already predicted in IF, but the
matching `jalr x0, 0(ra)` return waits until EX and always redirects there,
creating a frontend bubble on every function return in CoreMark.

A small return-address predictor is a better predictor-category bet than
another replay-table rewrite. It targets real IPC by predicting a currently
unpredicted but common control-flow class, while the state can be kept tiny
and updated non-speculatively from already-resolved EX instructions.
hypothesis:
Add a very small return-address stack predictor for canonical RISC-V return
instructions. The IF stage should still use the current static predictor
for legal backward branches and aligned direct JALs, with the same shared
static target adder and the same replay table. In addition, when the fetched
instruction is a canonical return (`JALR`, `funct3 == 0`, `rd == x0`,
`rs1 == x1` or `x5`, immediate zero) and the return stack is non-empty,
predict the next PC from the return-stack top.

Keep the return stack non-speculative. Do not push or pop when IF merely
decodes an instruction. Instead, have EX report resolved, valid,
non-trapping call/return events back to IF on internal wires. Push `pc + 4`
when a resolved direct JAL writes x1 or x5. Pop when a resolved canonical
return retires through the JALR path. Because calls have a function body
before their return executes, an EX-time push is still early enough to
predict ordinary returns, and avoiding speculative IF updates eliminates
rollback machinery.

Add prediction-target metadata only as needed to repair JALR predictions
safely. Branches can keep outcome-only repair because their target is
deterministic from the instruction and PC, and direct JALs can keep the
existing "redirect only if not predicted" behavior. For predicted returns,
carry the 32-bit return-stack target through IF/ID and ID/EX. In EX, a JALR
should redirect when it was not predicted or when the resolved JALR target
differs from the carried predicted target. Misaligned jump targets remain
trapping and suppress redirects exactly as today.

This must not change replay-table geometry, live-imem priority,
fill-bypass behavior, MRU replacement, static backward-branch prediction,
direct-JAL prediction, load-use interlocks, forwarding priority,
pending-store behavior, memory traps, divider protocol, writeback timing,
top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0 retirement ordering,
channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder illegal defaults, or
CoreMark timing markers.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should improve by removing EX-redirect bubbles
from correctly predicted returns. LUT4 should remain below the current
target if the stack is kept small, for example two or four entries, and the
extra target metadata is used only for JALR repair. The intended phase-2
Pareto win is higher CoreMark iter/s with no LUT4 regression beyond the
3500 limit and no correctness-contract change.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Added a 32-bit `predicted_target` payload to the IF/ID and ID/EX bundles. Branches still use outcome-only repair, direct JAL keeps the existing predicted/unpredicted behavior, and the target payload is used for predicted JALR returns.
- Added a four-entry non-speculative return-address stack in `if_stage.sv`.
  - IF predicts only canonical returns: `jalr x0, 0(x1)` and `jalr x0, 0(x5)`.
  - Prediction uses the stack top only when the stack is non-empty.
  - The existing static backward-branch/direct-JAL predictor and two-bank replay table are otherwise unchanged.
- Added EX-to-IF return-stack update wires.
  - Resolved, valid, non-trapping direct JAL calls to `x1` or `x5` push `pc + 4`.
  - Resolved, valid, non-trapping canonical JALR returns pop.
  - Updates are gated with `!stall` so a held EX instruction cannot update the stack repeatedly during a MEM wait.
- Updated JALR redirect repair so predicted JALR returns redirect only when the resolved target differs from the carried predicted target. Unpredicted JALR still redirects as before.

## Deviations

- Misaligned jump targets still do not redirect to the misaligned target. For the new predicted-return case, EX repairs an already-taken stale prediction back to `pc + 4` on a misaligned target so RVFI `pc_next` and the next retired PC remain consistent.
- The return stack is four entries deep. On overflow it drops the oldest entry by shifting and pushes the newest return address at the top.

## Concerns

- Very deep call nesting beyond four entries will lose the oldest return address and rely on EX repair when unwinding past stack capacity.
- Immediate return chains where a callee returns directly into another return may still miss because the RAS is intentionally updated only after EX resolution.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: blocked by the sandbox because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared checkout outside the writable root, and the script needs to remove staged files under `cores/auto-arch-researcher`.
- Same formal script/checks run from a writable temporary copy under `/tmp/auto_arch_ras_formal.mph1qb`: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Immediate-Bit EX Target Alignment
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 526.32
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3414 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The micro_opt slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto free
win: preserve CoreMark cycles while trimming a small amount of EX control
logic or improving median Fmax.

The current EX stage computes full branch and jump targets, then tests the
low two bits of those targets to decide whether a control-flow instruction
traps for misalignment. For conditional branches and direct JAL, the low
target bits do not need the adder result: this RV32I core has no compressed
ISA support, reset starts at a word-aligned PC, sequential fetch advances by
four bytes, IF predicts only aligned static branch/JAL targets, and EX
suppresses redirects for misaligned control-flow targets. Therefore every
reachable branch/JAL base PC has pc[1:0] == 0, and B/J immediates have an
implicit zero bit 0. The misalignment check for branch and direct JAL reduces
to the decoded immediate bit 1.

This is narrower than the earlier EX PC-adder sharing, branch-comparator
factoring, predictor alignment, replay-table, ALU, register-file, or
reset-structure experiments. It leaves all target adders in place for actual
redirect and RVFI pc_wdata generation, and changes only the trap predicate
feeding the EX redirect/control cone.
hypothesis:
In rtl/ex_stage.sv, replace branch and direct-JAL misaligned-target tests
that depend on full target low bits with equivalent immediate-bit tests.
For a taken conditional branch, compute:

`misalign_branch = in.ctrl.is_branch && branch_taken && in.imm[1]`.

For jumps, keep the existing JALR sum because JALR target alignment depends
on rs1 + imm after bit 0 is cleared. Direct JAL can use the immediate bit:

`misalign_jump = in.ctrl.is_jump && (in.ctrl.is_jalr ? jalr_sum[1] : in.imm[1])`.

Keep `branch_target`, `jump_target`, and `branch_next_pc` generation
unchanged for redirect_target and RVFI-visible pc_next. Keep the existing
trap behavior exactly: misaligned branch/JAL/JALR sets `is_illegal`, clears
`reg_write`, suppresses redirect, and reports `pc + 4` as the resolved next
PC.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical because no fetch policy,
branch direction rule, direct-JAL rule, replay behavior, stall interlock,
memory protocol, divider protocol, writeback rule, or retirement rule
changes. The intended Pareto win is a small LUT4 reduction and/or higher
median Fmax by removing the branch/direct-JAL target-adder low-bit compare
from the misalignment and redirect gating cone, while preserving the
already-met LUT target and every CLAUDE.md invariant.

changes.description:
Change only the misaligned control-flow predicate. Use `in.imm[1]` for
taken conditional branch target alignment and for direct-JAL target
alignment; keep JALR alignment based on `jalr_sum[1]` because JALR uses
rs1 + imm with bit 0 cleared. Preserve branch and jump target arithmetic,
branch prediction repair, redirect_target selection, misaligned-trap
control modifications, JAL/JALR link-data routing, divider
launch/completion, operand forwarding, EX/MEM valid/stall behavior, and
all RVFI-visible semantics.

Update comments only if useful to document that EX may check branch and
direct-JAL target alignment from decoded immediate bit 1 under the
word-aligned fetch-PC invariant. Do not change bundle fields, ALU
encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO
assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Updated `rtl/ex_stage.sv` so taken conditional branch target misalignment is checked with `in.imm[1]` instead of `branch_target[1:0]`.
- Updated direct JAL target misalignment to use `in.imm[1]`.
- Preserved JALR target misalignment from `jalr_sum[1]`, since JALR alignment depends on `rs1 + imm` after bit 0 is cleared.
- Left branch target, jump target, redirect target, branch prediction repair, trapped `pc_next`, and RVFI-visible target arithmetic unchanged.

## Deviations

- No RTL deviations from the hypothesis.
- I did not change `rtl/core_pkg.sv`; the existing comments already describe the relevant immediate and PC flow, and no bundle or architectural contract change was needed.

## Concerns

- No implementation concerns. The change relies on the stated invariant that reachable branch/JAL PCs are word-aligned and B/J immediates have implicit bit 0 cleared.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
